coredata.dc:title: Fossil-like pollen grains for construction of UV-responsive photochromic and fluorogenic dual-functional film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Wenning
Han, Lulu
Xu, Ting
Chao, Zhenhua
Liu, Ziyu
Zhang, Chong
Jia, Lingyun
coredata.dc:description: Natural pollen, one of the most fascinating biological materials in existence, exhibits chemical modifications and tunable optical property in the process of fossilization. Herein, we firstly fabricated nature-based fossil-like pollen grains (F-PGs) with UV-responsive optical properties through a facile concentrated H2SO4 treatment. The F-PGs featured incomplete carbonization property while remaining the UV-active alkyl aliphatic groups, which could initiate the carbonylation of F-PGs surface under UV irradiation, thus endowing the distinct UV dose-dependent photochromic and fluorogenic dual-function of F-PGs. With the sustainable, easy-preparation and cost-effective advantages, the F-PGs were used to construct dual-functional films. The F-PG films presented high photochromic contrast (ΔE* = 32) to serve as smartphone-readable quick response code-patterned UV sensors, which indicated the highest reported cumulative dose of UV irradiation (108 J cm−2, equivalent to 10 days of exposure to sunlight). In addition, the UV-irradiated F-PG films with the fluorogenic property also showed wide applications in one-step and flexible photo-reduction of various metal ions to nanoparticles. Collectively, the novel fossil-like pollen grains and functional films pave the way for utilization of natural pollen for UV-responsive applications and provide a new insight for transporting the renewable biomass into photochromic and fluorogenic materials in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nature pollen
UV responsive
Photochromic
Fluorogenic
UV sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental
Materials
Fabrication of fossil-like pollen grains (F-PGs) with concentrated sulfuric acid
Material characterizations
UV irradiation on F-PGs
Fabrication of F-PG film via surface spray-coating method
UV-responsive photochromic characterization of F-PG film
UV-responsive fluorogenic characterization of F-PG film
Photo-reduction of metal ions to nanoparticles by UV-F-PG film
Results and discussion
Fabrication and characterization of the incompletely carbonized F-PGs
UV-responsive photochromic performance of F-PG film
UV-responsive fluorogenic performance of F-PG film
Chemical changes in surface chemistry of UV-F-PGs
Photo-patterned F-PG film as a colorimetric UV sensor
Photo-reduction of metal ions to nanoparticles with fluorogenic UV-F-PG films
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary material
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Wenning Jiang : Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - Original Draft. Lulu Han : Writing - Review & Editing, Acquisition of the financial support for the project leading to this publication. Ting Xu : Visualization. Zhenhua Chao : Validation. Ziyu Liu : Resources. Chong Zhang : Validation. Lingyun Jia : Management and coordination responsibility for the research activity planning and execution, Acquisition of the financial support for the project leading to this publication.

The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51773026), the National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFC1103002), the Liao Ning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1907189), the Dalian Key Technology R&D Program (2018J11CY008), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (DUT20LAB121, DUT20YG107). The authors also thank Dr. S. Muyldermans (Vrije Universiteit Brussel) and Ms. Wei Yang (Chemical analysis and research center for fluorescence imaging at Dalian University of Technology) for the patient language editing and the generous help. Also sincerely thank to Dr. Wenjun Zhu (Dalian University of Technology) for the helpful discussion of the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showed the fabrication and UV-responsive property of F-PGs. (a) Fabrication of F-PGs and UV-F-PGs. Scale bar = 10 µm. The F-PGs and UV-F-PGs were applied in the fabrication of photo-induced pattern (b), quick response (QR)-patterned UV sensor (c), and solid photo-reductant to prepare metal nanoparticles (d).

The optical and morphology property characterization of F-PGs. (a) UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra and photographs of pre-PGs and F-PGs. Scale bar = 500 mm. (b) Photo-fluorescence spectra of pre-PGs and F-PGs at 405 nm excitation wavelength. The insets are the fluorescence and white light-merged photographs of pre-PGs and F-PGs. Scale bar = 10 µm. (c) Scanning electron micrographs (SEM) of F-PGs. The inset shows the hollow core of F-PGs.
Chemical characterization of F-PGs. (a) Atom ratio of pre-PGs and F-PGs calculated from the element analysis result. (b) FTIR characterization of pre-PGs and F-PGs. (c) 13C NMR spectrum of F-PGs.

UV-responsive colorimetric performance of the F-PGs. (a) Fabrication procedure of F-PG film. (b) SEM images of F-PG film. The inset is the zoom-in image. (c) Photographs of color gradient change over time under 20 mW m−2 UV irradiation. Scale bar = 500 mm. (d) UV–visible diffuse-reflectance spectrum and (e) color coordinates (L*, a*, b*) of F-PG films under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation for various durations. (f) Color change (∆E) of F-PG films under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation. (g) Colorimetric rate of F-PG film under the corresponding UV irradiation intensity as a function of time. (h) Photographs of color gradient change over time at 10, 20, and 30 mW m−2 UV irradiation. Scale bar = 500 mm.

UV-responsive fluorogenic response property of F-PGs. (a) Photo-fluorescence spectrum of UV-F-PGs after 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation for 90 min (b) Fluorescence images of UV-F-PGs under indicated wavelength exciton. (c) Typical CLSM images of UV-F-PG films under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation for various durations. Scale bar = 200 µm. (d) Average fluorescence intensity of UV-F-PG films as a function of UV irradiation time.

Chemical changes in surface chemistry of UV-F-PGs. (a) Element analysis, (b) FTIR characterization, (c) 13C NMR spectra, and (d) carbon structure distributions of F-PGs and 90 min UV-irradiated UV-F-PGs. (e) XPS peak-fitting for chemical binding of (left) carbon (C 1s) and (right) oxygen (O 1s) peaks for original F-PGs and 90 min UV-F-PGs. (f) Correlation between the C˭O binding proportions in C 1s and the optical change of F-PG film over UV irradiation time.

Erasable photo-induced patterns as a colorimetric UV sensor. (a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication of photo-induced pattern with F-PG film. (b) Photograph and (c) fluorescence images of the 2D photo-pattern under excitation wavelengths of 365 nm (left), 488 nm (middle), 545 nm (right), respectively. (d) Photo-patterned school badge gradually disappeared over time under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation. (e) A QR code-patterned UV sensor could be read by a smartphone. (f) The QR code pattern almost disappeared and became non-readable after exposure to sunlight through the window for 21 days.

Photo-reduction of metal ions to nanoparticles with UV-F-PG film. (a) Schematic diagram of the photo-reduction process of Au nanoparticles with UV-F-PG film. (b) TEM image of the produced Au nanoparticles. (c) Absorption spectra of HAuCl4 suspensions after photo-reduction reaction in the addition of UV-F-PG films with various UV irradiation durations. The inset showed the photograph of the HAuCl4 suspensions after photo-reduction with UV-F-PGs that were irradiated under gradually increased UV time (from left to right). (d) Absorption spectra (left) and TEM image (right) of AgNO3 suspensions after photo-reduction. The inset showed the photograph of AgNO3 suspensions before and after photo-reduction with UV-F-PG film. (e) Fluorescence images of the UV-F-PG films before and after the photo-reduction. Scale bar = 200 µm.
Chemical shifts and the area fraction of carbon resonances in 13C NMR spectrum of F-PGs.
Comparisons of total dose for complete discoloration among reported colorimetric UV sensors and F-PG film.

row.entry: Total dose for complete discoloration (J cm−2)
UV-responsive ink with organic dyes and photo-redox molecule

head.ce:title: Fossil-like pollen grains for construction of UV-responsive photochromic and fluorogenic dual-functional film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Imaging, School of Bioengineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Imaging, School of Bioengineering, Dalian University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Imaging, School of Bioengineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116023, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural pollen, one of the most fascinating biological materials in existence, exhibits chemical modifications and tunable optical property in the process of fossilization. Herein, we firstly fabricated nature-based fossil-like pollen grains (F-PGs) with UV-responsive optical properties through a facile concentrated H2SO4 treatment. The F-PGs featured incomplete carbonization property while remaining the UV-active alkyl aliphatic groups, which could initiate the carbonylation of F-PGs surface under UV irradiation, thus endowing the distinct UV dose-dependent photochromic and fluorogenic dual-function of F-PGs. With the sustainable, easy-preparation and cost-effective advantages, the F-PGs were used to construct dual-functional films. The F-PG films presented high photochromic contrast (ΔE* = 32) to serve as smartphone-readable quick response code-patterned UV sensors, which indicated the highest reported cumulative dose of UV irradiation (108 J cm−2, equivalent to 10 days of exposure to sunlight). In addition, the UV-irradiated F-PG films with the fluorogenic property also showed wide applications in one-step and flexible photo-reduction of various metal ions to nanoparticles. Collectively, the novel fossil-like pollen grains and functional films pave the way for utilization of natural pollen for UV-responsive applications and provide a new insight for transporting the renewable biomass into photochromic and fluorogenic materials in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Photochromic and fluorogenic materials have been attracting a great deal of attention due to their wide applications in anticounterfeiting [1], photo-pattering [2], UV sensor [3] and rewritable system [4,5]. So far, the photo-responsive materials are mainly based on synthetic rare-earth metal complexes [6,7], organic dyes [3,8] and photoisomerization molecules [9], which show common limitations of high cost, strict reaction control and environmental unsustainability. Thus, the quest for more cost-effective photo-responsive materials from natural and renewable sources has accelerated.

During billions of years of evolution, nature has created countless mysterious livings bearing precise structures and charming functions. Natural pollen, the most ancient carrier of the male gametes for plant reproduction featuring hierarchical outer exine, unique porosity and tunable mechanical properties, has been widely applied in drug delivery [10–12], microencapsulation [13,14], cancer cell capture [15], soft hydrogel [16] and humidity-responsive actuator [17]. Additionally, due to the advantages such as large specific area, low cost, reproducibility and wide availability, the natural pollen is also employed as bio-templates in catalysis [18], energy storage [19] and electrochemistry [20]. Beyond the well-developed physical structures, pollen exine is the most chemically resistant biomaterial, surviving as a fossil in geological sediments over millions of years [21]. Moreover, fossil pollen undergoes various chemical modifications and exhibits both colorimetric and fluorogenic properties in response to the surrounding environmental factors (such as UV irradiation, temperature, pressure, soil pH, etc.) [22], which endow the fossil pollen as the most ancient and sensitive indicator of historic climatic variation [23]. As a result of this, understanding and tuning the chemical composition of fossil pollen is crucial to ensure the effective utilization of pollen for developing environment-responsive natural materials. However, due to the scarcity and unavailability, fossil pollen has only been utilized in paleoenvironmental studies and petroleum exploration [24,25]. To our knowledge, there are few studies that have attempted to harness the attractive natural pollen for developing the photo-responsive materials with tunable optical properties.

Here, we firstly demonstrated nature-based fossil-like pollen grains (F-PGs) with controllable UV-responsive optical properties, and fabricated F-PG films with photochromic and fluorogenic dual-functions. The F-PGs were fabricated with a facile, scalable and rapid H2SO4 treatment, which simulated the dehydration process during natural fossilization. This treatment chemically increased the carbonization degree of pollen grains while retaining the original alkyl aliphatic groups. Under UV irradiation, the produced active alkyl radicals led to the carbonylation of F-PGs surface and further resulted in the distinct color change and the increase in photo-fluorescence emission of F-PG film. Considering these UV-responsive properties of F-PG film, we designed UV-induced patterns and quick response (QR) code-patterned UV sensors to evaluate the long-time cumulation of UV irradiation. Moreover, the UV-irradiated F-PG films with fluorogenic property could be used as a solid photo-reductant for the one-step and flexible fabrication of various metal nanoparticles ( Fig. 1). The results of this work provide a wide spectrum of possibilities for fabricating novel photochromic and fluorogenic materials with sustainable natural biomass.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Chrysanthemum pollen grains were purchased from Shumin Apiculture (China). Acetone (C3H6O, 99.5%) and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) were purchased from Sinopharm (China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%) was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company (China). Polyvinyl butyral (PVB), gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4.3H2O, 99.9%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99%) and cupric sulfate anhydrous (CuSO4, 99.95%) were purchased from J&K Chemical (China). Deionized water with a resistance of 18 MΩ cm was produced by a Milli-Q water purification system (USA). All chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fossil-like pollen grains (F-PGs) with concentrated sulfuric acid
ce:section.ce:para: The typical synthesis process of F-PGs was based on our previous study [15]. Briefly, 100 g of natural chrysanthemum pollen grains were firstly dispersed in 1 L of water and then filtered. The monodisperse pollen grains were stirred in 1 L of acetone under reflux for 6 h. After filtration, the pollen grains were stirred in 1 L of sodium hydroxide solution (6%, w/v) for 12 h. Following filtration and washing with water, the drained pretreated pollen grains (designated as pre-PGs) were suspended in 1 L of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) in a flask and gently mixed to form a homogeneous suspension, which was further stirred under reflux for 8 h. Thereafter, the pollen grains were collected by centrifugation and washed extensively with water and ethanol. Finally, the resulted hollow fossil-like pollen grains (designated as F-PGs) were dried at room temperature for 48 h in a vacuum dryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible/diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis/DRS) and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy were conducted by a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750S, Perkin-Elmer, USA) equipped with an integrating sphere. The photoluminescence spectra of the pollen samples were examined under a confocal fluorescence microscope at 405 nm excitation wavelength (FluoView™ FV1000, Olympus, Japan). The surface morphology was investigated under a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU8220, Hitachi, Japan). The particle size distributions were measured using a particle size distribution analyzer (Master Size 2000, Malvern Instruments, UK). The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were evaluated at 77 K on a Quadrasor-SI instrument (Quantachrome, USA). The surface areas were estimated by the conventional Brunauer–Emmet–Teller (BET) technique, while the pore volume and pore size distribution were determined by the density functional theory. The elemental compositions of the samples were analyzed by the VarioEL analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The water contact angles were measured using a drop shape analyzer (DSA25, Kruss, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an AXIS Ultra DLD (Kratos, UK) at a base pressure of 1 × 10 −9 Torr and a working pressure of 5 × 10−9 Torr with mono Al Kα radiation. The 13C cross-polarization/magic angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (13C NMR) were obtained by an Agilent DD2–500 MHz NMR spectrometer (Agilent, USA). TEM measurements were carried out using a transmission electron microscope (JEM- 2200FS, JEOL, Japan) at 200 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV irradiation on F-PGs
ce:section.ce:para: F-PGs (50 mg) were spread evenly on a plastic disposable petri dish (90 × 15 mm) using an airbrush and exposed to UV irradiation (20 mW cm−2) emitted by the CEL-PE300-3A solar simulator (Beijing Teach Jinyuan Technology, China) with a UVA filter (320–400 nm) for 90 min. The UV irradiated F-PGs were designated as UV-F-PGs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of F-PG film via surface spray-coating method
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a polyvinyl butyral (PVB) film was fabricated by dripping PVB ethanol solution (10%, w/v) on a flat glass substrate via spin-coating at a rotation rate of 4000 rev min−1 for 30 s in air, or via spreading PVB solution (5%, w/v) on the irregular plastic bottles. F-PGs suspended in the ethanol solution (25 mg mL−1) was then sprayed over the PVB film using an airbrush (U-STAR, A-119) at a nozzle height of 10 cm, a carrier gas pressure of 30 psi and a spraying time of 10 s. The obtained F-PG film was purged with N2 to remove the excess pollens and then blown-dried. The ratio of F-PGs coverage to PVB film was characterized as previously reported [15]. To ensure the accuracy of measurement, the F-PG films used in experiments exhibited more than 97% F-PGs coverage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-responsive photochromic characterization of F-PG film
ce:section.ce:para:
The F-PG films (2 × 2 cm2) were irradiated under the CEL-PE300-3A solar simulator with the UVA filter (320–400 nm). The irradiation intensity of 10, 20, and 30 mW cm−2 was calibrated by an optical power meter. After irradiation of different time and light intensity, the UV–visible/diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-Vis/DRS) of F-PG films were conducted with a Lambda 750 UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The Commission Internationale de l′Eclairage (CIE) parameters L*(lightness), a*(red/green coordinate), and b*(yellow/blue coordinate) of UV irradiated F-PG films were calculated with the UV-Vis/DRS from 360 nm to 750 nm [26]. At least five locations of each film were taken and each location was measured three times. The CIE parameters of the original F-PGs were indicated as L0*, a0*, and b0*. The overall color change ∆E was calculated using the following equation:
(1) ∆Et* = ((∆Lt*)2 + (∆at*)2 + (∆bt*)2)1/2
where t is the irradiation time, ∆Lt* = Lt*– L0*, ∆at* = at*– a0*, and ∆bt* = bt * – b0 *.

To study the kinetics of color change, the maximum color change during the UV irradiation is
∆
E
max
, and the logarithm of color change
∆
E
max
− ∆
E
t
∆
E
max
were plotted against t based on the following equation:
(2)
ln
∆
E
max
− ∆
E
t
∆
E
max
= − K t
where K is the slope of the best-fitted line, which represents the colorimetric rate of F-PG film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-responsive fluorogenic characterization of F-PG film
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence micrographs of UV-F-PGs irradiated for 90 min were taken under an Olympus CKX53 inverted fluorescent microscope. The photoluminescence spectrometry and corresponding typical fluorescence images of ordinary F-PG film and UV irradiated F-PG films (UV-F-PG film) were evaluated under a FluoView™ FV1000 confocal fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). The average fluorescent intensity of the obtained fluorescence images was analyzed with Image J software [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-reduction of metal ions to nanoparticles by UV-F-PG film
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the F-PG films (1 × 1 cm2) irradiated with UV light for different durations were placed into a 24-well plate. Then, 1 mL of 0.2 mM chloroauric acid hydrated solution, 1 M silver nitrate solution, or 1 M copper sulfate solutions were loaded in the well with the UV-F-PG films for the preparation of gold, silver or copper nanoparticles, respectively. The photo-reduction of UV-F-PG films and metal ions was carried out under irradiation of 50 mW cm−2 UVA light irradiation (Intelli-ray 400 Uvitron, USA) for 10 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Fabrication and characterization of the incompletely carbonized F-PGs
ce:section.ce:para: Pollen exine is composed of chemically inert biomacromolecule sporopollenin that is stable under alkaline and acidic conditions. To obtain the monodisperse pretreated pollen grains (pre-PGs), water washing, defatting, and alkali-treatment processes were performed to remove the surface-adhered residues and internal proteins. Afterwards, to simulate the dehydration and cycloaddition reaction in natural fossilization process, the widely used dehydrating agent concentrated sulfuric acid (98%, H2SO4) was employed to incompletely carbonize the pre-PGs as fossil-like PGs (F-PGs). After concentrated H2SO4 treatment for 8 h, the color of F-PGs changed from pale yellow to black-brown while autofluorescence disappeared ( Fig. 2a,b). Moreover, the SEM images showed that, after 8 h H2SO4 treatment, the F-PGs were etched as hollow micro-particles with an average diameter of 25 µm (Fig. 2c), and the integrity of PGs was well preserved even after the extended 24 h H2SO4 treatment (Fig. S1a,b in the Supporting Information). Importantly, due to the etching effect of H2SO4 treatment, the F-PGs possessed rougher surface and more mesoporous structure with a substantially increased specific surface area of 45 m2 g−1 compared with pre-PGs (Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information), indicating a potential large-surface-area advantage for light-harvesting of F-PGs.

The chemical composition of F-PGs after 8 h H2SO4 treatment was determined by element analysis, FTIR, and 13C NMR. The increase in C/O and C/H molar ratio of F-PGs indicated the dehydration process occurred during H2SO4 treatment ( Fig. 3a). Compared with the FTIR spectra of pre-PGs (Fig. 3b), the emerged stretching vibration bands of –SO3H (1023 cm−1 and 1170 cm−1) [28] and the elevated aromatic absorption band (1560 cm−1) [29] in F-PGs further demonstrated the occurrence of sulfurization and carbonization during H2SO4 treatment. The previous studies reported that unsaturated carbonyl groups in sporopollenin of natural PGs exine play a dominant role in forming various “fluorophores” and “chromophores”, resulting in yellow color and autofluorescence of natural pollen [30]. Hence, the significant decrease in the characteristic absorption peak of the ester group (RO-C˭O at 1737 cm−1) [28] in F-PGs (Fig. 3b) can be regarded as the main reason for the quenching of PGs auto-fluorescence, which is consistent with the previous studies [30,31]. Notably, FTIR results of the F-PGs showed that the alkyl groups (-CH2- and -CH3) were still retained and assigned at 2927 and 2855 cm−1. Similarly, the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 3c) further assessed that 48% of alkyl aliphatic groups ( Table 1, δ = 0–50 [32]) remained in the F-PGs, demonstrating the carbonization was incomplete. It has been reported that the polymer structure of sporopollenin in PGs contains large amounts of C16 aliphatic units as cross-linkages (53.7% of total carbon) [32]. Previous studies showed that the dehydration reaction of H2SO4 is initiated by eliminating the oxygen-containing groups [33]. Thus, the alkyl aliphatic groups (-CH2-) in C16 units which not adjacent to oxygen would be retained during the H2SO4 dehydration treatment [34]. In contrast, other biopolymers such as cellulose [35] and lignin [36] hardly contain any long-chain alkanes, thus they are completely carbonized without alkyl aliphatic groups preservation after H2SO4 treatment. Above all, for pollen grains, the H2SO4 treatment not only blackened the PGs and quenched their autofluorescence, but also retained the active alkyl aliphatic groups of PGs, which may endow the F-PGs with UV-responsive properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-responsive photochromic performance of F-PG film
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate functional F-PG films, a spray-painting method was conducted to sprinkle the F-PGs onto a PVB-casted planar substrate ( Fig. 4a) with more than 97% F-PGs coverage [15]. We first investigated the light absorption properties of F-PG film by UV–visible/diffuse reflectance measurement (UV–Vis/DRS). The F-PGs exhibited a strong UV light absorption ranging from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. S3a in the Supporting Information), which is the main part of the solar UV irradiation spectrum [37].

Under continuous irradiation of 20 mW cm−2 UV light (6 times of solar UV intensity [38]), the F-PG film displayed a visible color change from black-brown to yellow-brown as UV-F-PG film (Fig. 4c), which was also verified by the enhanced reflectance intensity in the visible region (Fig. 4d). To quantify the photochromic properties of UV-F-PGs, the standard 1976 CIE color coordinates a*, b*(chromaticity coordinates) and L* (lightness coordinate) [26] were calculated and the ∆E* indicated the overall colorimetric degree from F-PG film to UV-F-PG film. The gradual increase in L* and b* represented the F-PG film turning brighter and yellower (Fig. 4e) under UV irradiation. While the visible light (400–800 nm) showed a negligible effect on the F-PGs visible color (Fig. S3b in the Supporting Information), which demonstrated the selective UV-responsive photochromic property of F-PGs. The ∆E* curve of F-PG film under 20 mW cm−1 UV irradiation (Fig. 4f) improved as a function of irradiation time and was well fitted to the pseudo-first-order kinetic law (R2 = 0.997). The F-PG films presented the maximum ∆E* value of 32, which was remarkably larger than several reported photochromic sensors, such as natural wood surface (∆E* = 16) [39], light driven PVDF fibers (∆E* = 15) [40] and UV-responsive polyvinyl alcohol nanofibers (∆E* = 4.5) [3], proving the excellent photochromic property of F-PG film. Otherwise, the photochromic response time of F-PG film spanned 3–90 min at 20 mW cm−1, revealing that the F-PG film could indicate the total UV irradiation dose of 108 J cm−2, equivalent to 10 days of exposure to sunlight [41]. And the UV-F-PG film exhibited no break or fracture after 12 h irradiation, proving the stability of F-PG films after long-time UV radiation (Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information). To further demonstrate the relationship between photochromic rate and irradiation intensity, the F-PG films were also irradiated under 10–30 mW cm−2 UV light, respectively (Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information), and the colorimetric rate K of F-PG films increased from 0.0064 to 0.141 min−1 with the higher irradiation intensity (Fig. 4g). Furthermore, the corresponding color linear changes (Fig. 4h) also reflected the UV dose-dependent responsivity of F-PGs, which allowed the F-PGs to be designed as a colorimetric UV sensor. Besides, the 20 mW cm−2 UV light exhibited moderate irradiation intensity which led to the obvious color gradient change of F-PG film in 90 min, therefore it was used to fabricate UV-responsive F-PGs and films in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-responsive fluorogenic performance of F-PG film
ce:section.ce:para: Accompanying the visible photochromic property, the F-PG film also exhibited a prominent UV-responsive fluorogenic character. After 90 min UV irradiation, the fluorescence spectrum of UV-F-PGs under a 405 nm excitation showed an emission peak at 465 nm and a stronger emission peak ranging from 515 to 540 nm ( Fig. 5a), enabling the observation of green, yellow, and red fluorescence under a fluorescent microscope (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of UV-F-PG films increased as a function of UV irradiation time (Fig. 5c and Fig. S6 in the Supporting Information), which fitted the pseudo-first-order kinetic law (R2 > 0.99) (Fig. 5d) and showed a good correlation with the total color changes (Fig. S7 in the Supporting Information). Thus, the above results revealed that the F-PG films showed great UV-responsive photochromic and fluorogenic dual-functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical changes in surface chemistry of UV-F-PGs
ce:section.ce:para: After UV irradiation for 90 min, the obtained UV-F-PGs kept their initial microscale morphology with the contact angle decreasing from 74º to 23º (Fig. S8 in the Supporting Information), indicating that the chemistry modification occurred on the surface of UV-F-PGs. The element analysis results showed that the 90 min UV irradiation yielded an increase of oxygen content and a reduction of carbon content in F-PGs ( Fig. 6a). The precise chemical changes of UV-F-PGs were further examined by FTIR and 13C NMR. After UV irradiation, the typical absorption band of the carbonyl group (R2C˭O) at 1690–1708 cm−1 [42] significantly emerged, while the characteristic absorption of aromatic structure and alkyl groups decreased (Fig. 6b). As verified by 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 6c), 5% of alkane group and 14% of aromatic structures disappeared in the UV-F-PGs (Table S1). Whereas, after 90 min UV irradiation, the proportions of C-OH ( δ = 65), HO-C˭O group ( δ = 167) and R2C˭O group ( δ = 197) increased by 1.4, 1.9 and 1.4-fold, respectively (Fig. 6d). The above data indicated that the UV irradiation-induced surface modification and resulted in the significant increase of oxygen-containing functional groups and, in turn, decreased C-C and C˭C groups. Moreover, these chemical changes were also proved by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of UV-F-PGs (Fig. S9 in the Supporting Information), which exhibited a blue-shift from 405 nm to 335 nm after UV irradiation, indicating the increased n–π* transition of unsaturated carbonyls [43] and the decreased alkene conjugated system [44].

Subsequently, aiming to better clarify the relationship between the chemical composition and the optical behaviors of F-PGs films, XPS analysis of F-PGs under UV irradiation for different durations (0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90 min) was performed (Fig. 6d and S10a,b in the Supporting Information). Overall, the C˭O binding proportions in both C 1s and O 1s peaks increased as a function of UV irradiation time (Fig. S10c,d in the Supporting Information), and yielded a positive correlation to the photochromic and fluorogenic change of F-PG film (Fig. 6f, R2 > 0.9). Collectively, the correlated data from multiple independent methods provided that, the optical behaviors of F-PGs under UV irradiation are mainly attributed to the accumulation of carbonyl groups.

It has been reported that UV-induced formation of carbonyl-based colorimetric groups through photo-radical reaction is the main reason for the photo-yellowing of organic polymers, such as polystyrene [43] polyterpene [45] and lignin [46]. While, due to the induction effect and steric hindrance of phenyl group [43], this photo-radical reaction preferentially begins in the aliphatic chains to produce primary active radicals [47], which played a crucial role in the photo-yellowing process [48]. Thus, UV-responsive photochromic and fluorogenic dual-function of F-PGs could be explained as the photo-radical reaction which relies on the participation of aliphatic chains. Firstly, the UV-induced hydrogen extraction occurs in the aliphatic chains of F-PGs, leading to the formation of active alkyl radicals. Then the radicals further react with oxygen to produce carbonyl-based colorimetric groups or attack the aromatic phenol to facilitate the formation of yellow quinone products.

To further confirm the vital function of aliphatic chains in UV-induced chemical changes of F-PGs, completely carbonized pyrolysis-treated pollen grains (pyro-PGs) with alkyl-free carbon structures were used as a contrast (Fig. S11a and Table S2 in the Supporting Information). The pyro-PGs showed no discoloration after 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation for 90 min (Fig. S11b in the Supporting Information). This corresponded with the previous study that the transformation of phenol to yellow quinone products did not occur in absence of alkyl radicals [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-patterned F-PG film as a colorimetric UV sensor
ce:section.ce:para: UV irradiation accumulation is related to the photoaging of human skin and thereby leads to the risk of skin cancer [50]. In addition, UV irradiation accumulation accelerates photodegradation and photooxidation of the cosmetic, pharmaceutical, food, chemical, and other items [51]. Several photo-current sensing systems have been reported to evaluate the instantaneous UV light intensity, but they cannot present the long-term cumulative dose [38] and often require complex spectroscopic instruments and interdigitated electrode sensor [52,53]. To address the above issues, we employed the UV-responsive F-PG films to create photo-induced patterns as a multifunctional UV sensor to indicate the cumulative UV irradiation dose.

Firstly, the fabrication of photo-induced patterns was investigated with F-PG film using a transparent and black photomask under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation ( Fig. 7a). As the transparent part allowed the UV light to pass through, the irradiated F-PGs under the corresponding region turned yellow. Meanwhile, the opaque black part prevented the photo-discoloration and thereby kept the original color. Moreover, the obtained 2 D yellow and black-brown photo-induced pattern could transfer to the fluorescence pattern under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 7b, c). Furthermore, the black-brown patterns on the F-PG films could be erased by UV light. As shown in Fig. 7d, the fabricated school badge-pattern gradually turned yellow under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation for 30 min (36 J cm−2) and faded thoroughly after 90 min irradiation (108 J cm−2), indicating that the UV-erasable property of F-PGs-fabricated photo-induced patterns was proportional to the cumulation of UV irradiation.

Based on this UV-erasable property of the photo-induced patterns, the F-PG film was used to construct the quick response (QR) code-patterned UV sensor for indicating the cumulative UV irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7e, the as-prepared QR code pattern on the plastic bottle was conveniently read by a smartphone (Fig. 7e and Video 1 in the Supporting Information), and remained readable after the bottle was placed in a dark chamber for 21 days (Fig. S12 in the Supporting Information). Whereas, for the bottle exposed in parallel to the sunlight through a window for 21 days, the QR code pattern was erased and no longer recognized by a smartphone (Fig. 7f), indicating the plastic bottle has received a UV irradiation dose of more than 108 J cm−2. In comparison with the previously reported organic dye-based UV sensors only used for measurement of the instantaneous UV light intensity [38,54–59], this photochromic UV sensor derived from natural pollen not only displayed sustainable, easy-preparation and cost-effective advantages, but also could indicate a longer-time cumulative dose of UV irradiation for informing on the preservation condition and durability of items ( Table 2). Moreover, the QR patterns of the UV sensor would conveniently provide more information when linked to web pages or posters, thereby further expanding its application field. In addition, the F-PGs-constructed UV sensor was surface-independent and therefore applicable to different material surfaces and shape items.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126944.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-reduction of metal ions to nanoparticles with fluorogenic UV-F-PG films
ce:section.ce:para: After being used as UV sensor, the F-PG film changed to UV-F-PG films with fluorogenic property, which could be used and further served as excellent electron donors for the reduction of metal ions. To prove the photo-reduction property of UV-F-PG film, a HAuCl4 solution and the 90 min UV-irradiated UV-F-PG film ( Fig. 8a) were placed under 20 mW cm−2 UV irradiation for 10 min without any other catalysts. After the photo-reduction, the color of the HAuCl4 solution changed from faint yellow to wine red and was accompanied by the formation of spherical Au nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 13 to 50 nm (Fig. 8b). Whereas, in absence of the UV-F-PG film, no nanoparticle was formed in the HAuCl4 solution under UV irradiation (Fig. S13 in the Supporting Information).

Furthermore, to investigate the relationship between the photo-reduction activity and the fluorogenic property of UV-F-PG films, the F-PG films were exposed under UV irradiation from 0 min to 90 min to enhance the fluorogenic property. As shown in Fig. 8c, as the fluorescence of UV-F-PG films got stronger, the HAuCl4 solution turned from light pink to wine-red with an increased characteristic absorbance peak at 535 nm, indicating the formation of a larger quantity of Au nanoparticles [60,61]. Furthermore, the UV-F-PG films also showed wide applications in the facile and flexible fabrication of various metal nanoparticles (Fig. 8d and S14 in the Supporting Information). The mechanism of UV-F-PG film on photo-reduction of Au ions to Au NPs is the photo-induced electron transfer process (PET process) [62–64], whereby an electron is transferred from the photoexcited state of UV-F-PGs to Au3+ to produce reduced Au nanoparticles, while quenching the fluorescence of UV-F-PG film (Fig. 8e), corresponding to the previous reports [65–67]. By contrast, the non-fluorescent F-PG films hardly reduced the Au NPs (Fig. 8c), and the UV-F-PG film showed no photo-reduction property in dark (Fig. S15 in the Supporting Information), which further confirmed the photo-reduction property of UV-F-PG films relied on the fluorescence emission.

Compared with the common reducing agents (such as photo-reductive carbon quantum dots (CDs) or sodium citrate) that are hardly separated from the nanoparticle products, the UV-F-PG film served as an easily separable photo-reduction and showed potential in preparing well-defined metal materials for fundamental studies and applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we developed a nature-based fossil-like pollen material with UV-responsive optical properties via a facile H2SO4 treatment and fabricated dual-functional films. Our approach has the following merits: Firstly, the H2SO4 treatment for natural pollen is facile and rapid and can be easily scaled up. Secondly, the concentrated H2SO4 delicately controls the chemical composition of sporopollenin, leading to the incomplete carbonization of natural pollen exine and retaining original alkyl aliphatic groups. Thirdly, the functional F-PG films were easy-prepared and applicable to different material surfaces on a large scale regardless of composition and shape. We systematically demonstrated that the UV-induced active alkyl radicals resulted in the formation of carbonyl-based products in F-PG surface, which further endowed the F-PGs with UV-induced photochromic and fluorogenic functions. Moreover, for the first time, we applied the naturally derived F-PGs into the QR code-patterned film as UV sensor, enabling it to be read by smartphone and to evaluate the long-time cumulation of UV exposure. Moreover, the fluorogenic UV-F-PG films could be further used as a solid photo-reductant for preparing various metal nanoparticles. These results expand the potential applications of natural pollen-derived materials and offer new insight into the design of photochromic and fluorogenic materials with renewable biomasses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Wenning Jiang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - Original Draft. Lulu Han: Writing - Review & Editing, Acquisition of the financial support for the project leading to this publication. Ting Xu: Visualization. Zhenhua Chao: Validation. Ziyu Liu: Resources. Chong Zhang: Validation. Lingyun Jia: Management and coordination responsibility for the research activity planning and execution, Acquisition of the financial support for the project leading to this publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51773026), the National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFC1103002), the Liao Ning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1907189), the Dalian Key Technology R&D Program (2018J11CY008), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (DUT20LAB121, DUT20YG107). The authors also thank Dr. S. Muyldermans (Vrije Universiteit Brussel) and Ms. Wei Yang (Chemical analysis and research center for fluorescence imaging at Dalian University of Technology) for the patient language editing and the generous help. Also sincerely thank to Dr. Wenjun Zhu (Dalian University of Technology) for the helpful discussion of the XPS analysis.
